title: 2025 07 16 - HE
displayTitle: 2025-07-16 - Health Committee - Notice of motion to approve the Misuse of Drugs (Classification and Presumption of Supply) Order 2025
topic: Notice of motion to approve the Misuse of Drugs (Classification and Presumption of Supply) Order 2025
description: 16 July 2025 | Health Committee Notice of motion to approve the Misuse of Drugs (Classification and Presumption of Supply) Order 2025 Ministry of Health Tania Jones, Manager, Regulatory Practice and Analysis Chris James, Group Manager, Medsafe Diana Yeritsyan, Senior Solicitor, Health Legal Nathalie Bere-Adams, Senior Advisor, regulatory Practice and Analysis Briefing from Bronda Smith Bronda Smith Petition of Gareth Lowndes: Increase resources for the Health and Disability Commissioner Gareth Lowndes Briefing from Arthritis New Zealand Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora Dr Sarah Clarke, National Clinical Director Primary and Community Care Dr Peter Gow, Clinical Director & Rheumatologist Andrew Bary, Manager Pharmacy, Planning, Funding and Outcomes
descriptionSummary: Notice of motion to approve the Misuse of Drugs (Classification and Presumption of Supply) Order 2025. Ministry of Health. Tania Jones, Manager, Regulatory Practice and Analysis

Uh, the order is required to classify substances under the misuse of Drugs Act 1975.

Substances or controlled drugs in New Zealand are scheduled under motor and categorizes either Class A, B, or C, depending on the risk of harm they pose to individuals in society with Class A being the highest risk of harm. The substances that are being recommended for scheduling in this order are based on recommendations that are made by the Expert Advisory Committee on drugs.

The A A CD is a statutory committee, which is set up under the misuse of Drugs Act to make recommendations to the Minister of Health on drug classification issues. The EACD is made up of a range of experts, which who have relevant areas of expertise, such as pharmacology, toxicology, drug and alcohol treatment, psychology, community medicine, as well as having experts in public health, the app appropriateness and safety of pharmaceuticals and their availability to the public, uh, and from border control, and also representatives from police ministry for justice,

and also a person who represents the views of consumers and drug treatment services. DACD consider a range of aspects when considering substances such as the likelihood or evidence of abuse, prevalence, levels of consumption, seizure trends, and potential appeal of vulnerable populations, specific effects such as, uh, pharmacological, psychoactive, and toxicological effects, risk to public health, any therapeutic value potential to cause death, and the ability to create dependence, international classification and experience.

This order includes recommendations on the classification of 22 individual substances, specifically three, fentanyl analogs as Class A controlled drugs usually to very high risk of harm, three, synthetic cathinones and five synthetic cannabinoids is Class B controlled drugs due to a high risk of harm, three other synthetic cannabinoids and three benzodiazepines is Class C controlled drugs, um, as they're considered to pose a moderate risk of harm.

In addition, five substances which you use in the manufacturer of amphetamine and methamphetamine, are being sh scheduled as precursor substances. And the order also takes the opportunity to classify substances related to tramadol, which is currently classified as a Class C substance. The order is also sets the quantity at which they presume to be for supply.

The proposed new classifications will also allow New Zealand to meet international obligations as party to several United Nations Drug conventions, which have listed many of these substances. Scheduling substances under motor provides police and customs with powers to disrupt supply. It's not aimed at in criminalizing individual users.

These substances have been considered has, there has been an increase in prevalence and misuse of these substances in domestic and or international jurisdictions. None of these substances included in this order are used therapeutically. Uh, but should that change, there is a mechanism under the act for doctors to prescribe these for their patients.

Uh, and it's preventable again, with intervention at midlife. And there's a hormonal component there as well. Women are twice as likely as men to suffer from dementia, and that's estimated, that's growing as you know, and estimated to cost the health system, you know, close to $6 billion by 2050.

The changes that are the precursors to dementia start to happen inside the brain decades before they become evident. Mr. Allen. Very good to have you here. Thank you for coming in person to present your petition. Thank you. Uh, we have you here for 10 minutes. Uh, if you wanna leave some time for members, ask questions. Sure. You may. I'll hand that time to you now. Welcome to the health committee. Thank you very much. Uh, good morning chair members of the Health Select Committee, thank you for the opportunity to speak in front of you today regarding the critical state of the Health and Disability Commissioner's office.

You'll have received my written submission, and I hope, I hope you've had the chance to review it today. I wanted to highlight the most pressing issues and underscore why it's not just a periodic problem, but a deeply human one impacting countless New Zealanders. Today. I'm also speaking on behalf of my sister who can't be here today, but is watching the live stream from her home in Melbourne In January, 2022, our mother, Anna Marie Lambs, died from post ERCP Pancreatitis, following a routine procedure at Middlemore Hospital,

I immediately filed complaints with the hospital and the HTC seeking answers and accountability while Middlemore swiftly completed their investigation within a few months and recommended changes. Our family has now been waiting for answers from the HEC for over three years. 1,267 days to be exact. Tragically, our father, who was equally, uh, desperate for those answers passed away just four months later.

His determination to understand what happened fuels my commitment to be here today. Our family's prolonged wait is not an isolated incident. It's a stark illustration of a systemic failure that traps other families and medical practitioners in similar distress. The HCC is mandated to uphold patient rights and ensure accountability, however, it's fundamentally under-resourced, overwhelmed, and operating with a severely compromised capacity.

Consider these facts mainly from the h many from the HCCs own data and public statements. In the 20 22 23 year, the HT C received over 6,200 complaints. My own OIA requests confirm the average investigation time is blown out to 893 days during a Health Select Committee meeting in May, 2020.

Um, in May, 2024, the HC commissioner herself revealed that a vital $2 million funding request to replace their antiquated Lotus Notes IT system was declined by the previous government. This is a system so outdated that it's mere mentioned, elicited laughter from members of this very committee during that meeting, a clear testament to how demonstrably unfit it is for purpose.

I understand the HCC has now conducted an RFP to move to Microsoft Dynamics, but they've had to scramble to find this budget from their existing already strained operating costs. Making matters worse, the HCC faces significant financial blow. The 2024 budgets were a $3 million funding cut. This represented a 16.5% reduction to the overall budget.

While the recent 2025 budget documents do mention an increased funding for the Health and Disability Commissioner to improve complaint resolution and care standards, and a one of $1 million has been allocated specifically to address the backlog. This new funding does not come close to offsetting the larger cut from the prior budget. The gravity of these challenges is underscored by the recent resignation of the Aged Care Commissioner, who was asked of her early exit reflected frustrations with the role or its associated resourcing.

She responded, and I quote, as you would expect, I have to carefully answer that. I think it's a challenge to BA balance the pure complaints work under the legislation with the focus on prevention and quality improvement for older people. But it's not impossible. It's just a challenge, and it's one that I've loved her. Guarded by talent comments, highlight the immense pressure on even the most senior leadership within the HT C to balance the legislative duties with limited resource.

A challenge that is evidently taking its toll on impacting the HCCs ability to fulfill its prevention and quality improvement mandate. This brings me to a point of profound frustration, one that highlights the core issue in the very same three years I've been waiting for the HT C to conclude its investigation. I, as a private citizen, have personally initiated and have achieved more tangible progress to advocating for systemic changes within the very health system that failed my mother and continues to fail other families.

This is included seeing new a RC procedural guidelines implemented at MIDDLEMORE and A RCP audits introduced. I'm now actively working directly with Health and Zed to understand how these updated guidelines can be rolled out nationally to all hospitals. It is absurd that I have been able to drive these crucial improvements while the HEC remains stuck in slow processes.

I want to be clear that this is not a criticism of the commissioner or her dedicated staff. I understand they're making efforts internally to approve efficiency. However, as one senior member of the HEC staff told me, these internal shifts, while positive are sufficient, are, are insufficient given. And I quote, the growing gap between the number of complaints we receive and the number we can close within the current resources.

My concern is with the broken system itself, the chronic underfunding, the outdated tools and the lack of political will to properly empower this essential watchdog. I'm calling our members of all parties to take action rather than kicking the can down the road for someone else to deal with. This is not something that will score political points, but it will make a difference to people's lives.

And surely that's what matters. It's utterly embarrassing for the government and the HCC that significant systemic, systemic changes now depend on the efforts of individual citizens. What makes us even more egregious is that my ability to affect these changes was heavily reliant on the extensive network of contacts and reputation that my mother built during her 30 years of dedicated service to counties, Manco Health.

Gout, arthritis is common, cruel, crippling, and costly and occurs when serum blood urate levels become high enough that crystals form in the joints with severe inflammation and severe pain. New Zealand has some of the highest gout rates in the world, particularly counties, Monaco, around 5.7% of New Zealanders are affected, but it's much higher in Pacific people at 14.8% and Maori at 8.5%.

But gout is also curable if treated early with optimal doses of Allopurinol or other urate lowering treatment to reduce the level of serum urate, which is also known as uric acid to a target level of less than 0.36 million miles per liter. A high prevalence of gout is found in Maori and Pacific peoples, not because of what they eat or drink, but because they have a genetic difference which reduces the excretion of urate in the urine, particularly when associated with obesity.

This means that there are higher levels of serum urate in these populations, and gout occurs at a younger age with a great impact on their working life and subsequent socioeconomic factors. Pacific peoples in Maori are nine times and five times more likely to be hospitalized with gout compared to the European population.

Maori Pacific patients do not have to give up things which are culturally important to them, such as Ana when their serum ator is controlled, and this can be used as a positive motivator to encourage adherence. I won't see it a Norwegian GP and pathy tell a patient, I can control your gout without you having to give up.

Being a Maori gout is associated in about one third of patients with diabetes, kidney disease, and cardiovascular problems. And painful acute gout is often the first sign that these asymptomatic diseases are present so that they can be identified and treated. These conditions can be more difficult to treat in patients with uncontrolled gout because anti-inflammatory drugs for acute gout, such as volta can aggravate kidney disease and cardiovascular disease, and prednisone can make diabetes worse.

Uncontrolled gout is also associated with strokes, excuse me, and premature death from cardiovascular disease. Recent national data shows that although Allopurinol was prescribed at least once and 60% of patients with count, only 44% are taking it regularly, and only 5% of patients are taking more than 300 milligrams daily.

A higher dose is often necessary because half the patients on Allopurinol do not achieve target urate levels. Mm-hmm. The solution to this undertreatment is the up titration of Allopurinol guided by serum urate levels. With recent studies showing the effectiveness of pharmacists in this regard and the systematic research review, both internationally and in New Zealand, there are key elements which promote success in current gout programs.

The stop gout program in Northland has an automated prescription, which takes into account whether the patient has kidney disease or diabetes with escalation of the dose. Using free blister pack pharmacists provide education and health support. Workers or kayna provide community support and encouragement to continue with the medication.

Urate levels can be checked at each visit and the allopurinol dose increased if necessary. The other program of which I was recently involved was the county's gout buster program in which pharmacists had four training sessions, including medical training and cultural training by Pacific and Maori educators.

A critical feature of this program was the use of electronic software, which enabled communication with gps and notification of urate levels, as well as providing text messages to patients to provide encouragement and appointment reminders. This program also included point of care testing with finger prick for urate levels and up titration according to standing orders from the gp.

In both of these programs in which 75% of patients were either Maori or Pacific fecals, the rate of regular allopurinol uptake was around 70%. Feedback from the patients in both programs was extremely positive as it was from pharmacists and the GPS who responded to the survey. Other programs around New Zealand include elements of the gout bus from stop gout programs, which can be customized to local needs when they are purinol reclassifications gazed trained pharmacists will be able to dispense continuation of supply

for up to 12 months outstanding orders from the GP, and will be able to form up titration required to achieve the urate targets.
